Headline: Nelson Mandela Released From Prison
Clue 1: The Berlin Wall had fallen just months before
Clue 2: South Africa was still three years from its first democratic election
Context Card: Nelson Mandela had spent 18 of his 27 years imprisoned on Robben Island, a bleak outcrop in Table Bay. By the time he walked free on February 11th, 1990, he had become the most famous political prisoner in the world. Within four years, he was president.

Headline: The Apollo 11 Moon Landing
Clue 1: Woodstock was just weeks away
Clue 2: The Boeing 747 had not yet carried a single passenger
Context Card: Neil Armstrong's first steps on the Moon were watched by an estimated 600 million people — one-fifth of the world's population. The lunar module was called Eagle. Armstrong had only 30 seconds of fuel remaining when he found a landing spot. He called it 'magnificent desolation.'

Headline: The Titanic Sinks on Maiden Voyage
Clue 1: The Panama Canal was still under construction
Clue 2: Fenway Park opened the same week
Context Card: The Titanic was carrying some of the world's wealthiest people and hundreds of immigrants seeking a new life in America. It struck the iceberg at 11:40 PM and sank in under three hours. The nearby RMS Carpathia rescued 705 survivors. The disaster led to major changes in maritime safety regulations.

Headline: JFK Assassinated in Dallas
Clue 1: Martin Luther King Jr. had just delivered his most famous speech that summer
Clue 2: The Beatles would land in America for the first time within three months
Context Card: Lee Harvey Oswald fired three shots from the sixth floor of the Texas School Book Depository on November 22nd, 1963. Kennedy was pronounced dead at 1:00 PM. Two days later, Oswald was shot by nightclub owner Jack Ruby in the Dallas Police Department basement — live on national television. The truth of what happened that day has never fully left public debate.

Headline: World Wide Web Invented
Clue 1: The Exxon Valdez had just run aground in Alaska
Clue 2: Nintendo's Game Boy went on sale that same spring
Context Card: Tim Berners-Lee's original proposal at CERN was returned with the note: 'Vague, but exciting.' He wanted a way for physicists to share documents across different computers. The first web page went live in 1991, and Berners-Lee never patented his invention — he gave it away for free, insisting the web should belong to everyone.

Headline: Chernobyl Nuclear Disaster
Clue 1: The Space Shuttle Challenger had been lost earlier that same year
Clue 2: Maradona's 'Hand of God' goal happened weeks later at the World Cup
Context Card: On April 26, 1986, Reactor 4 at Chernobyl exploded during a low-power test. The fire released radioactive material 9 kilometers into the atmosphere. Soviet authorities evacuated Pripyat (population 49,000) 36 hours later. The final death toll remains disputed — from 31 immediate deaths to estimates of thousands of cancer cases from radiation exposure. The destroyed reactor was finally enclosed in a giant steel sarcophagus in 2016.

Event: D-Day: Allied Forces Land at Normandy
Headline: D-Day: Allied Invasion of Normandy
Clue 1: Roosevelt was serving an unprecedented third term as US president
Context Card: On June 6th, 1944, the Allies launched the most complex military operation in history. Paratroopers had already dropped behind enemy lines by the time the first landing craft hit the beaches at 6:30 AM. The cost was enormous — over 4,000 Allied soldiers died on D-Day alone — but within a year, Germany had surrendered.

Headline: First iPhone Released
Clue 1: The final Harry Potter book was published that same summer
Context Card: Steve Jobs unveiled the iPhone on January 9, 2007, calling it 'a revolutionary product.' It didn't go on sale until June, but it immediately changed what people expected from mobile devices. The initial model had no App Store — that came in 2008. By 2011, the iPhone had made Apple the most valuable company in the world.

Headline: The Watergate Break-In
Clue 1: Nixon had just returned from his historic visit to China that same year
Context Card: The five men arrested inside the Democratic National Committee headquarters at 2:30 AM were carrying wiretapping equipment and $2,300 in sequential hundred-dollar bills. A security guard named Frank Wills noticed tape on a door lock and called the police. What began as a third-rate burglary unraveled into the greatest political scandal in American history, ending a presidency two years later.

Event: The Atomic Bomb Drops on Hiroshima
Headline: Atomic Bomb Dropped on Hiroshima
Clue 1: Mussolini had been executed by partisans just months earlier
Context Card: The Manhattan Project had cost two billion dollars and employed hundreds of thousands of people — most of whom had no idea what they were building. On August 6th, 1945, the result was dropped on Hiroshima. Three days later, a second bomb fell on Nagasaki. Japan surrendered on August 15th. The atomic age had begun.

Headline: Fall of the Soviet Union
Clue 1: The World Wide Web had just become publicly available that August
Context Card: On December 26, 1991, the Supreme Soviet of the USSR ratified the Belovezh Accords, formally ending the Soviet Union. What had been the world's largest country by area splintered into 15 independent nations. For 69 years, the Soviet experiment had shaped global politics. Its end brought both hope for a new world order and anxiety about nuclear weapons in unstable new states.

Headline: The Wright Brothers' First Flight
Clue 1: The first Tour de France was held that same summer
Context Card: The Wright Brothers made four flights at Kitty Hawk on December 17th, 1903. The first lasted just 12 seconds and covered 120 feet. By the end of that day, they had flown a total of 59 seconds. Orville would later say that the sensation of flight never fully wore off on him — it just kept getting better.

Headline: Lindbergh Flies Solo Across the Atlantic
Clue 1: Jazz ruled the speakeasies and hemlines were rising
Context Card: The Spirit of St. Louis was so loaded with fuel at takeoff that it barely cleared the telephone wires at the end of the runway. Charles Lindbergh landed at Le Bourget at 10:22 PM on May 21st, 1927, and was immediately mobbed by the crowd. He became the most famous man in the world overnight — and remained so for the rest of his life, for better and worse.

Headline: The Cuban Missile Crisis
Clue 1: Marilyn Monroe had sung 'Happy Birthday' to the president that summer
Context Card: For thirteen days in October 1962, the world came closer to nuclear war than it ever has before or since. Soviet ships carrying additional missiles turned back at the US naval blockade. Soviet leader Khrushchev eventually agreed to remove the missiles in exchange for a US pledge not to invade Cuba and a secret agreement to remove American missiles from Turkey. The crisis was resolved — but only barely.

Event: Rosa Parks Refuses to Give Up Her Seat
Headline: Rosa Parks Refuses to Give Up Her Seat
Clue 1: Rock and roll was new, Disneyland had just opened its gates
Context Card: Rosa Parks was not simply tired from work when she refused to move — she was tired of giving in. She had been trained at the Highlander Folk School, a center for civil rights activism. Her arrest on December 1st, 1955 was strategic: the NAACP had been looking for a test case. The resulting boycott lasted 381 days and ended with the Supreme Court ruling bus segregation unconstitutional.

Headline: The Challenger Disaster
Context Card: Engineers at Morton Thiokol had warned NASA the night before launch that O-rings could fail in temperatures below 53°F — and it was 28°F at Cape Canaveral that morning. Their objections were overruled. The Rogers Commission that investigated the disaster famously included physicist Richard Feynman, who demonstrated the O-ring failure with a glass of ice water at a press conference.

Headline: The September 11 Attacks
Context Card: On September 11, 2001, nearly 3,000 people were killed in a series of coordinated terrorist attacks. The FBI quickly opened an investigation called PENTTBOM. Within days, President George W. Bush had signed the USA PATRIOT Act. The attacks reshaped American foreign policy, airport security, and domestic surveillance in ways still felt today.

Headline: Attack on Pearl Harbor
Context Card: The attack on Pearl Harbor lasted just under two hours but destroyed or damaged 19 Navy ships and 328 aircraft. Of the 2,403 Americans killed, nearly half died aboard the USS Arizona, which sank in nine minutes. Japan had intended to deliver a formal declaration of war before the attack, but the message arrived late — turning a military strike into something Americans would never forgive.

Headline: First Human in Space
Clue 1: The Berlin Wall went up later that same summer
Clue 2: A new American president had just taken office promising a New Frontier
Context Card: Yuri Gagarin orbited Earth in Vostok 1 on April 12, 1961, completing one orbit in 108 minutes. At 27, he became the first human to see Earth from space. He parachuted from his capsule before it landed — ejection at 7km was standard procedure. A city in Kazakhstan was renamed Baikonur in his honor, and his face became a symbol of Soviet achievement.

Headline: French Revolution Begins
Clue 1: George Washington had just been inaugurated as the first US president
Clue 2: Mozart was composing his last operas in Vienna
Context Card: On July 14, 1789, several hundred Parisians stormed the Bastille, a medieval fortress that had become a symbol of royal tyranny. They freed the seven prisoners inside and killed the governor. The event became the defining moment of the French Revolution and is now commemorated annually as Bastille Day, France's national holiday.

Headline: Declaration of Independence Signed
Clue 1: Adam Smith published The Wealth of Nations the same year
Clue 2: Captain Cook was on his third and final voyage
Context Card: Thomas Jefferson drafted the Declaration of Independence in seventeen days. Congress made 86 changes to his draft, cutting roughly a quarter of the text — including a passage condemning the slave trade. The final document was signed by 56 men, many of whom knew they were signing what could become their death warrant.

Headline: MLK's "I Have a Dream" Speech
Clue 1: The Beatles released 'Please Please Me' earlier that year
Clue 2: A nuclear test ban treaty was signed that same summer
Context Card: The March on Washington was organized in just two months. King's most famous lines — 'I have a dream' — were partly improvised; gospel singer Mahalia Jackson shouted 'Tell them about the dream, Martin!' from behind him on the stage. The prepared text had no mention of dreams.

Headline: First Email Sent
Clue 1: Intel had just released its first microprocessor
Clue 2: The Pentagon Papers were published in The New York Times that same year
Context Card: Ray Tomlinson sent the first email in 1971, choosing the @ symbol to separate the user name from the computer name. The message itself was largely forgettable — something like 'QWERTYUIOP.' But the @ sign became Tomlinson's lasting contribution to digital communication, now used in billions of email addresses worldwide.

Headline: Google Founded
Clue 1: The Lewinsky scandal dominated American headlines
Clue 2: Titanic was the year's biggest film at the box office
Context Card: Larry Page and Sergey Brin met at Stanford in 1995 and initially didn't get along. Their search algorithm, PageRank, ranked websites by their links rather than keywords. Their first office was a garage rented from a friend. The name 'Google' came from 'googol,' a mathematical term for 1 followed by 100 zeros.

Headline: Amazon Founded
Clue 1: Nelson Mandela had just been elected president of South Africa
Clue 2: The first PlayStation launched in Japan that December
Context Card: Jeff Bezos left his hedge fund job in 1994 to start an online bookstore, originally called Cadabra. He chose the name Amazon because it sounded 'exotic and different,' and the Amazon River was the biggest river he could think of. He raised $1 million from his own parents — the minimum viable investment to start. By 2020, Amazon had a market cap of $1.7 trillion.

Headline: DNA Double Helix Discovered
Clue 1: Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay summited Everest weeks later
Clue 2: Elizabeth II had just been crowned Queen of England
Context Card: Watson and Crick published their double helix model in Nature on April 25, 1953. Their famous line: 'It has not escaped our notice that the specific pairing we have postulated immediately suggests a possible copying mechanism for the genetic material.' Rosalind Franklin's Photo 51, which showed the helix structure, was critical — but she died in 1958 and never shared the Nobel Prize.

Headline: HIV/AIDS First Identified
Clue 1: MTV broadcast its first music video that August
Clue 2: The IBM personal computer went on sale for the first time
Context Card: The CDC's Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report on June 5, 1981, documented five young gay men in Los Angeles with a rare pneumonia. By the end of 1981, there were 159 reported cases. The disease wouldn't be called AIDS until 1982, and the HIV virus wouldn't be identified until 1984. It would eventually kill 35 million people worldwide.

Headline: First Human Heart Transplant
Clue 1: The Summer of Love had just swept through San Francisco
Context Card: Christiaan Barnard performed the first human heart transplant in Cape Town on December 3, 1967, replacing the heart of Louis Washkansky with that of a young woman killed in a car accident. The operation took nine hours. Washkansky survived just eighteen days before dying of pneumonia — but Barnard had proved it could be done, and within a year, more than a hundred transplants followed worldwide.

Headline: Polio Vaccine Announced
Clue 1: Disneyland opened its gates in Anaheim that same summer
Context Card: When Jonas Salk's polio vaccine was declared 'safe, effective, and potent' on April 12, 1955, church bells rang across America. Polio had paralyzed an average of 35,000 people each year during the early 1950s. Salk famously refused to patent the vaccine — when asked who owned it, he replied, 'The people. Could you patent the sun?'

Headline: Penicillin Discovered
Clue 1: Mickey Mouse made his first appearance on screen that same year
Context Card: Alexander Fleming returned from holiday to find a petri dish contaminated by a mold that had killed the surrounding bacteria. He nearly threw it away. It took another decade and two other scientists — Florey and Chain — to turn his observation into a usable drug. By D-Day, there was enough penicillin to treat every wounded Allied soldier on the beaches of Normandy.

Headline: Instagram Launched
Clue 1: The iPad had just debuted earlier that same year
Context Card: The app gained 25,000 users on its first day — before it even existed on Android. Built in eight weeks, it nearly launched under the name 'Burbn' before a pivot stripped everything except photo filters and sharing. Within two years, a billion-dollar acquisition made it the most expensive purchase-per-employee in tech history.

Headline: YouTube Founded
Clue 1: Hurricane Katrina footage flooded the platform months after launch
Context Card: The first video ever uploaded — 18 seconds of a man at the San Diego Zoo — set the tone for what would become the world's second-largest search engine. Three former payment-company employees built it above a pizzeria, and within 18 months sold it for $1.65 billion. The site now serves over a billion hours of video daily, more than broadcast television and Netflix combined.

Headline: Wikipedia Launched
Clue 1: The dot-com bubble had just burst, leaving server space cheap
Context Card: Its founder originally envisioned an expert-written encyclopedia called Nupedia, which produced exactly 12 articles in its first year. The pivot to open editing was meant as a feeder project, but within weeks the wiki had outpaced its parent. A 2005 Nature study found its science articles contained roughly the same error rate as Encyclopædia Britannica.

Headline: Euro Currency Introduced
Clue 1: A millennium's last quiet revolution, sealed before the champagne corks flew
Context Card: The Euro was introduced as an accounting currency on January 1, 1999, with physical notes and coins following on January 1, 2002. Twelve countries abandoned their national currencies — including the Deutsche Mark, French Franc, and Italian Lira — for the first time in history. The transition was initially chaotic in some countries, with ATM glitches and confusion at stores.

Headline: Nintendo Game Boy Released
Clue 1: Cold War concrete was crumbling in Europe while children everywhere stared down at their palms
Context Card: Competitors offered color screens, backlighting, and superior processors — and lost. The device's designer deliberately chose outdated screen technology because it consumed so little power that four AA batteries lasted 30 hours. Bundled with a puzzle game from the Soviet Union, it sold 118 million units and proved that portability mattered more than performance.

Headline: Black Tuesday Stock Crash
Clue 1: Talkies had just killed the silent film star; now silence fell on the trading floor
Context Card: The ticker tape ran so far behind actual trades that brokers were selling stocks without knowing their real price. Sixteen million shares changed hands that day — a volume record that stood for nearly 40 years. The market wouldn't return to its pre-crash peak until 1954, a full quarter-century later.

Headline: First Photograph Taken
Context Card: The exposure required roughly eight hours of direct sunlight, producing a grainy view of rooftops and a courtyard from an upstairs window in Burgundy. The inventor had been experimenting with light-sensitive bitumen for nearly a decade, failing repeatedly before this single surviving image. It now lives in a climate-controlled case at the University of Texas, so faded it can only be seen when lit at a precise angle.

Headline: First Telegraph Message Sent
Context Card: The message traveled 40 miles from the Capitol in Washington to a railroad depot in Baltimore in less than a second — a speed that made newspaper editors openly anxious about the future of their profession. Within a decade, wire stretched across the continent; within two, it crossed the Atlantic Ocean floor.

Headline: Printing Press Invented
Context Card: The technology itself wasn't new — China had movable type four centuries earlier — but the innovation was combining it with a converted wine press, oil-based ink, and a metal alloy that could survive thousands of impressions. Within 50 years, an estimated 20 million volumes had been produced across Europe, more than all the scribes of the previous millennium combined.

Headline: American Civil War Begins
Clue 1: Abraham Lincoln had been inaugurated just weeks before the first shots
Clue 2: The Pony Express was still carrying mail across the western frontier
Context Card: The 34-hour bombardment killed no one on either side — the only fatality came during the surrender ceremony, when a cannon exploded. The fort's commander had personally trained several of the Confederate officers now firing on him. Four years and 620,000 deaths later, he returned to raise the same flag he'd been forced to lower.

Headline: Spanish Flu Pandemic Begins
Clue 1: The Great War was grinding through its final year in Europe
Clue 2: Ragtime was giving way to early jazz in American dance halls
Context Card: It killed more people in 24 weeks than HIV/AIDS killed in 24 years, yet wartime censorship kept it out of headlines in combatant nations — only neutral Spain reported freely, which is how a Kansas-origin virus got a Spanish name. The second wave struck healthy adults aged 20-40 hardest. Global death estimates range from 50 to 100 million, making the true toll permanently unknowable.

Headline: Elvis Presley's First TV Appearance
Clue 1: The Interstate Highway System had just been authorized by Congress
Clue 2: The Hungarian Revolution was making front-page news that autumn
Context Card: CBS cameras were ordered to film from the waist up, but the damage was already done — his appearance weeks earlier had drawn the largest television audience in history. He earned $50,000 for three appearances, a staggering sum for a 21-year-old truck driver from Mississippi. The host privately called him 'a decent, fine boy' while publicly declaring the performance unsuitable for family television.

Headline: First McDonald's Franchise Opens
Clue 1: Disneyland opened its gates in Anaheim that same summer
Clue 2: Rosa Parks refused to give up her bus seat in Montgomery months later
Context Card: The franchising salesman who built the empire wasn't the founder — he was a 52-year-old milkshake machine vendor who walked into a San Bernardino hamburger stand and saw the future. The original brothers had already perfected their 'Speedee Service System,' serving a burger in 30 seconds. He bought them out for $2.7 million; their handshake deal for ongoing royalties was never honored.

Headline: Disneyland Opens
Clue 1: James Dean's final film was released that same year
Clue 2: The Salk polio vaccine had just been declared safe months earlier
Context Card: Opening day was such a disaster that insiders still call it 'Black Sunday' — temperatures hit 100°F, asphalt softened under women's heels, and counterfeit tickets doubled the crowd. A plumbers' strike forced a choice between working toilets and working water fountains; the creator chose toilets. The park recouped its entire $17 million construction cost within seven weeks.

Headline: First Barbie Doll Released
Clue 1: Alaska and Hawaii became states that same year
Clue 2: Fidel Castro had just seized power in Havana weeks earlier
Context Card: She was inspired by a German novelty doll originally marketed to adult men as a gag gift. The creator's male business partners all argued that mothers would never buy their daughters a doll with breasts; 300,000 sold in the first year. Her full name — Barbara Millicent Roberts — was never printed on a box.

Headline: Pac-Man Released
Clue 1: The designer wanted to create something women and couples would play on dates
Clue 2: The US boycotted that summer's Olympics in the host city
Context Card: The original Japanese name was changed for Western markets because distributors feared vandals would alter the first letter. It earned $2.5 billion in quarters within its first 15 months. The kill screen on level 256, caused by an integer overflow, was not beaten legitimately until 1999.

Headline: Super Mario Bros Released
Clue 1: The North American console market had collapsed two years prior and retailers refused to stock games
Clue 2: Live Aid concerts raised millions for Ethiopian famine relief that same summer
Context Card: The game was designed as the pack-in title for a console that retailers initially refused to carry. Its creator had no formal programming training and composed the music himself. The game sold 40 million copies — without internet distribution — and single-handedly resurrected a home entertainment industry that analysts had declared permanently dead.

Headline: Tetris Released
Clue 1: Its creator worked at the Soviet Academy of Sciences and initially received no royalties
Clue 2: George Orwell's dystopian year had just passed with surprising calm
Context Card: The licensing rights became the subject of the most byzantine legal battle in gaming history — the Soviet government, a British software company, and multiple American publishers all claimed ownership simultaneously. Its designer earned nothing from the game's first decade of global sales. Cognitive scientists later discovered it literally changes brain structure, thickening the cortex in regions governing spatial reasoning.

Headline: PlayStation Launched
Clue 1: The console was born from a failed partnership with its future rival's cartridge-based system
Context Card: It began as a CD-ROM add-on for a competitor's console, but the partnership collapsed in a public betrayal at a trade show. The jilted partner channeled that anger into a standalone machine that used cheaper disc media, undercut the competition by $100, and sold over 100 million units — inaugurating a new era of 3D gaming that made sprites feel instantly obsolete.

Headline: Xbox Announced
Clue 1: The September 11 attacks delayed the console's European launch by months
Context Card: The machine was essentially a stripped-down PC running a custom operating system, which horrified gaming purists. Internal memos reveal the company lost an estimated $125 on every unit sold, treating the entire venture as a $4 billion bet to control the living room. Its online service, bundled with an Ethernet port when competitors still used dial-up, would eventually reshape how the entire industry thought about multiplayer gaming.

Headline: First iPod Released
Clue 1: Portable MP3 players already existed, but this one used a tiny hard drive from a Japanese manufacturer
Context Card: Critics mocked the $399 price tag — 'no wireless, less space than a Nomad, lame,' read the most famous review. The device accounted for less than 2% of the company's revenue in its first year. Within six years it would generate half the company's income and lead directly to the product that would make it the world's most valuable corporation.

Headline: Netflix Streaming Launches
Clue 1: The first iPhone shipped that same summer, giving streaming a mobile future
Context Card: The streaming library at launch contained just 1,000 titles — less than a single Blockbuster store. The company had offered to sell itself to its brick-and-mortar rival for $50 million three years earlier and been laughed out of the room. That rival filed for bankruptcy three years after streaming launched; the streamer is now worth more than $150 billion.

Headline: Spotify Launches
Clue 1: The global financial crisis was unfolding as the service launched in Sweden
Context Card: It launched in invitation-only mode in a single European country, paying fractions of a cent per stream while the music industry was still prosecuting teenagers for illegal downloads. Major labels initially licensed their catalogs only because they were desperate — CD sales had fallen by half in a decade. The model musicians mocked as 'paying in pennies' now generates more revenue for the recording industry than CDs ever did at their peak.

Headline: Facebook Launched
Clue 1: Gmail launched in invitation-only beta that same year
Context Card: Registration required a valid university email address, and the site spread campus by campus — a deliberate scarcity strategy that created desperate demand at schools not yet invited. Within 24 hours of launching at each new university, more than half the student body had signed up. The founder was 19 years old, running the servers from his dormitory room.

Headline: Twitter Launched
Clue 1: A 140-character whisper that turned every phone into a printing press
Context Card: The original prototype was built in two weeks as a side project at a podcasting startup, and the first message — 'just setting up my twttr' — had all the grandeur of a throat-clearing cough. The 140-character limit was dictated by SMS technology, not philosophy. It would take an earthquake in Haiti, an uprising in Tehran, and an Arab Spring to reveal what a short-message platform could actually become.

Headline: Snapchat Launched
Clue 1: Born in a world where every photo was forever, promising that nothing had to be
Context Card: Every investor said an app built on impermanence was fundamentally worthless. A major social network offered $3 billion in cash to acquire it; the 23-year-old CEO declined. The feature everyone assumed was designed for sexting turned out to be what teenagers actually wanted: a way to communicate without building a permanent, searchable archive of their adolescence.

Headline: Bitcoin White Paper Published
Clue 1: Lehman Brothers had collapsed weeks before, and trust in banks was at a generational low
Context Card: The nine-page paper was posted to an obscure cryptography mailing list and received almost no attention. Embedded in the code of the first-ever block was a headline from The Times of London about bank bailouts — a quiet political statement disguised as a timestamp. The author vanished completely in 2011, leaving behind roughly one million tokens now worth tens of billions, never moved from their original addresses.

Headline: Ethereum Launches
Context Card: Its creator was a 21-year-old college dropout who decided the original digital currency was too limited — like a calculator when the world needed a computer. He funded the project through a public token sale that raised $18 million in digital currency. The platform's name was chosen from a list of science fiction concepts found on Wikipedia.

Headline: First NFT Minted
Context Card: The first known experiment linked a short animation to a blockchain entry and declared it a unique, ownable digital object — a concept that sounded absurd to anyone who understood how copying a file works. The technology languished in obscurity for years before a $69 million auction made the art world pay attention in 2021. The inevitable crash wiped out 95% of values within two years.

Headline: Columbus Reaches the Americas
Context Card: The navigator died believing he had reached Asia, never acknowledging the continental landmass his voyages had revealed to European mapmakers. The Taíno people he encountered had inhabited the Caribbean for at least 1,500 years. Within 50 years of first contact, disease, enslavement, and violence had reduced the Taíno population from an estimated 250,000 to fewer than 500.

Headline: Magna Carta Signed
Clue 1: The Crusades were still being fought in the Holy Land
Clue 2: Oxford University had already been teaching for decades
Context Card: The meadow at Runnymede was chosen because neither side trusted the other enough to meet indoors. King John sealed the charter under duress, then immediately asked the Pope to annul it — which he did within ten weeks. The document failed as a peace treaty but survived as the foundation of constitutional law across the English-speaking world.

Headline: Shakespeare Born
Clue 1: Elizabeth I had recently been crowned queen
Clue 2: Galileo was a newborn in Pisa
Context Card: Born in a market town best known for its wool trade, the playwright received a solid grammar school education but never attended university — a fact his rivals never let him forget. He would go on to invent over 1,700 English words, from 'assassination' to 'lonely.' His father made gloves; his son died at eleven.

Headline: Star Wars Released
Clue 1: Jimmy Carter was in his first year as US president
Clue 2: The Apple II personal computer went on sale that same year
Context Card: Twentieth Century Fox was so certain the film would fail that it let the director keep merchandising rights — a decision that would cost the studio billions. Shot partly in the Tunisian desert, where a nearby Libyan military operation nearly shut down production, it earned $461 million worldwide and redefined what a movie could sell.

Headline: Jaws Released
Clue 1: The Watergate scandal had just forced a president to resign
Clue 2: Saturday Night Live premiered on NBC that same fall
Context Card: The mechanical shark malfunctioned so often that the young director was forced to hide it, showing only glimpses — a constraint that accidentally invented modern suspense filmmaking. Beaches across America emptied that summer despite record heat. The film's success created the template every studio still follows: wide release, massive marketing, summer launch.

Headline: E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial Released
Clue 1: The Falklands War was being fought that same spring
Clue 2: Michael Jackson's Thriller album was released later that year
Context Card: The film was born from loneliness — the director conceived it while imagining an imaginary friend after his parents' divorce. M&M's famously declined a product placement deal, so the production used Reese's Pieces instead, tripling the candy's sales overnight. It became the highest-grossing film in history, a record it held for eleven years.

Headline: Jurassic Park Released
Clue 1: The European Union was formally established that same year
Clue 2: Nirvana's In Utero album was released weeks later
Context Card: The novel's team pivoted from animatronics to an untested technique — computer-generated imagery for living creatures. The result — six minutes of CGI dinosaurs in a two-hour film — convinced every studio in Hollywood that practical effects were finished. The paleontology department at many universities saw enrollment double within a year.

Headline: The Matrix Released
Clue 1: The Y2K bug had the world bracing for digital collapse
Clue 2: The euro currency was introduced to financial markets that January
Context Card: The directors pitched the film with a comic book because no studio could visualize what they were describing. Every lead actor in Hollywood turned down the role before it was offered to a star best known for action comedies. The 'bullet time' effect required 120 still cameras firing in sequence — borrowed from a 19th-century photographer who used it to study galloping horses.

Headline: Avatar Released
Clue 1: Barack Obama had just been inaugurated as US president
Clue 2: The world was deep in the worst financial crisis since the Great Depression
Context Card: The director waited fourteen years to make the film because the technology he needed did not exist yet. When it finally opened, it earned a billion dollars in just seventeen days. Its pioneering 3D technology was supposed to transform cinema forever; within five years, audiences had largely abandoned the format.

Headline: Sony Walkman Released
Clue 1: Margaret Thatcher had just become Britain's first female prime minister
Clue 2: The Soviet Union had recently invaded Afghanistan
Context Card: The device was rejected by every division inside the company before one engineer championed it. Its co-creator was so impatient with the prototype that he dunked it in an aquarium — when bubbles rose, he declared there was still space inside to make it smaller. It sold 200 million units and turned headphones from studio equipment into street fashion.

Headline: VHS Format Introduced
Clue 1: The United States was celebrating its bicentennial
Context Card: The format war between VHS and its rival Betamax had nothing to do with quality — Betamax was technically superior. VHS won because its tapes could record for two hours, long enough to capture a full movie, while Betamax initially maxed out at one. The adult film industry's early adoption of VHS may have tipped the balance, though the companies involved have always denied it.

Headline: CD Player Released
Clue 1: The Falklands War dominated the news that spring
Context Card: The disc's diameter — 12 centimeters — was allegedly chosen so it could hold all of Beethoven's Ninth Symphony without interruption. Early players cost more than $700, and only about fifty albums were available at launch. The format would eventually kill the vinyl record, then be killed itself by the MP3.

Headline: Kodak Camera Introduced
Clue 1: Jack the Ripper was terrorizing London's East End
Context Card: The founder's slogan was 'You press the button, we do the rest' — and he meant it literally. The camera came preloaded with a 100-exposure roll; when finished, customers mailed the entire device back to the factory. It democratized photography by removing every technical barrier, turning a specialist craft into a household habit.

Headline: Polaroid Camera Released
Clue 1: The Berlin Airlift was keeping a city alive that same year
Context Card: The inventor's three-year-old daughter asked why she couldn't see the photo he had just taken of her — and that question launched a billion-dollar company. The first camera weighed nearly four pounds and produced sepia-toned prints that had to be coated with fixative by hand. Andy Warhol would later make the instant photograph an art form, carrying one everywhere he went.

Headline: Digital Camera Invented
Clue 1: The Vietnam War had just ended with the fall of Saigon
Context Card: The prototype weighed eight pounds, took 23 seconds to record an image, and produced a 0.01 megapixel black-and-white photograph. The engineer who built it presented his invention to corporate leadership, who told him to bury it: the company's entire business was built on selling film. Three decades later, that same company filed for bankruptcy because digital photography had destroyed its market.

Headline: Ford Model T Released
Clue 1: The Wright brothers' first flight was still recent memory
Context Card: The assembly line did not start with the car — it started with the price. The founder was obsessed with making a car cheap enough for his own factory workers to buy, and the moving assembly line was simply the method that got the price from $850 down to $260. By the time production ended, half of all cars on American roads were the same model.

Headline: First Commercial Airplane Flight
Clue 1: The old world was about to tear itself apart
Context Card: The flight lasted 23 minutes, covered 21 miles across a bay, and the only passenger was the mayor of the destination city, who had won the seat at auction. The pilot made the trip twice daily for four months before the airline folded — there simply were not enough passengers. It would take another decade before commercial aviation became a real industry.

Headline: Sputnik Launched
Clue 1: Leave It to Beaver was America's picture of itself
Context Card: The satellite was the size of a beach ball and carried no scientific instruments — it could only transmit a simple radio pulse. But that beep, picked up by amateur radio operators worldwide, proved that the rival superpower had rockets powerful enough to reach any city on Earth. Within a year, the stunned response produced NASA, a new science curriculum for American schools, and the modern concept of the technology race.

Headline: Hubble Space Telescope Launched
Clue 1: The wall between East and West had just crumbled
Context Card: The telescope was delivered to orbit with a flaw in its primary mirror — a manufacturing error of just 2.2 micrometers, about one-fiftieth the width of a human hair. For three years it sent back blurry images. Astronauts installed corrective optics during a spacewalk, and the telescope went on to fundamentally change humanity's understanding of the age and size of the universe.

Headline: Mars Pathfinder Landing
Context Card: The rover was the size of a microwave oven and was designed to last seven days. It lasted eighty-three. Named through a student essay contest, the little machine analyzed rocks and sent back 2.3 billion bits of data, proving Mars had once been warm and wet. The mission cost less than the production budget of a Hollywood blockbuster released that same summer.

Headline: Gutenberg Bible Printed
Context Card: The printer was nearly bankrupt before the first Bible rolled off his press — he had borrowed heavily and his investor eventually sued him, seizing the equipment. The original print run was about 180 copies, of which 49 survive today, each worth tens of millions of dollars. Within fifty years, an estimated twenty million volumes had been printed across Europe, ending the Church's monopoly on knowledge.

Headline: Fall of the Western Roman Empire
Context Card: The final emperor was a teenager, installed as a puppet by his father, a Germanic general who actually held the power. The barbarian king who deposed him did not even bother to claim the title — he simply sent the imperial regalia to Constantinople and declared the western office unnecessary. The eastern half of the empire would continue for nearly another thousand years.

Headline: Great Fire of London
Clue 1: The Great Plague had devastated the same city just a year before
Clue 2: Isaac Newton was a young student developing his theory of gravity
Context Card: The fire began in a bakery on Pudding Lane shortly after midnight and burned for four days, destroying 13,200 houses, 87 churches, and old St Paul's Cathedral. Remarkably, only six verified deaths were recorded. The architect appointed to rebuild the city designed 51 new churches, including a cathedral that took 35 years to complete.

Event: The Beatles' First US TV Appearance
Headline: The Beatles on Ed Sullivan
Clue 1: The president had been assassinated just weeks before
Clue 2: Martin Luther King Jr. would receive the Nobel Peace Prize later that year
Context Card: When The Beatles landed at JFK Airport on February 7, 1964, they were met by 3,000 screaming fans — the largest welcome in airport history to that point. They appeared on The Ed Sullivan Show ten days later, watched by 73 million viewers. Within weeks, Beatlemania had completely reshaped American pop music and youth culture.

Headline: Woodstock Festival
Clue 1: Humans had walked on the moon just weeks earlier
Clue 2: Richard Nixon was in his first year as president
Context Card: The festival was supposed to happen in a different town entirely — the original site pulled the permit a month before the event. A dairy farmer agreed to lease his land, and within weeks his alfalfa field became a temporary city with no fences and no way to collect tickets. The promoters lost over a million dollars. The farmer said he would do it again.

Headline: Live Aid Concert
Clue 1: A hole in the ozone layer was discovered that same year
Clue 2: Nintendo released its first home console in North America that October
Context Card: The concert was organized in ten weeks — an impossibly short timeline for an event broadcast live to 1.9 billion people across 150 countries. The London and Philadelphia stages ran simultaneously, connected by satellite. It raised $127 million for famine relief, but its lasting impact was proving that television could turn a concert into a global event and shame governments into action.

Headline: MTV Launches
Clue 1: The first Space Shuttle mission launched that same spring
Clue 2: Prince Charles married Lady Diana that summer
Context Card: The first video ever played was a deliberate joke — a song about how television had disrupted an earlier medium. At launch, the channel could only be received in a few thousand homes in New Jersey. Record labels initially refused to provide videos, so the same handful of clips ran on heavy rotation for months. Within three years, an artist's visual image had become as important as their sound.

Headline: Napster Launches
Clue 1: The Y2K bug dominated headlines that December
Clue 2: The Sopranos had just premiered on HBO
Context Card: Two college freshmen built the service in a dorm room, and within a year it had more users than most countries have people. The recording industry sued, calling it the biggest threat to copyright since the photocopier. The legal battle shut it down, but the damage was done — an entire generation had learned that music could be free, and the industry spent the next decade trying to unlearn that lesson.

Headline: iTunes Store Opens
Clue 1: The US had invaded Iraq weeks earlier
Clue 2: The Human Genome Project was completed that same spring
Context Card: The music industry had spent four years suing teenagers and losing the public relations battle when a tech company proposed a radical idea: make buying music easier than stealing it. The store sold one million songs in its first five days. Within six years, it was the largest music retailer in the United States, and CDs were relics.

Headline: The Berlin Wall Falls
Clue 1: The Tiananmen Square protests had been crushed just months before
Clue 2: The first episode of The Simpsons aired that December
Context Card: For 28 years, Checkpoint Charlie was the defining symbol of the Cold War. On November 9th, 1989, a confused bureaucrat announced that East Germans could cross freely — and thousands flooded through. No one had planned the announcement. The wall fell because the system was already crumbling.

Headline: World War I Begins
Clue 1: The Panama Canal had just opened for business
Clue 2: Charlie Chaplin made his first film appearance that same year
Context Card: The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo on June 28, 1914, set off a chain reaction of alliance obligations. What was supposed to be a short war became a four-year conflict that killed 17 million people and destroyed four empires: the German, Austro-Hungarian, Ottoman, and Russian empires.
